recommendations: Break cycles and reduce dependence on the hottest modules.
summary: Modules or services depend too heavily on each other, so a small change in one area can break many others.
title: Tight Coupling

recommendations: Clarify package ownership and narrow cross-boundary dependencies.
summary: The system does not clearly define what responsibility belongs to each component.
title: Unclear Service or Module Boundaries

recommendations: Split overly broad modules into more focused units.
summary: A module or service handles too many unrelated responsibilities.
title: Low Cohesion

recommendations: Separate domain logic from persistence and transport concerns.
summary: Business logic, infrastructure code, UI behavior, and data access are mixed together.
title: Poor Separation of Concerns

evidence: Chatty or highly consumed interfaces: none
recommendations: Simplify and stabilize heavily consumed interface surfaces.
summary: Interfaces are inconsistent, overly complex, too chatty, or leak internal implementation details.
title: Bad Interface / API Design

recommendations: Assign a clear owner for each shared data target.
summary: Multiple services or modules read and write the same data without a clear owner.
title: Data Ownership and Source-of-Truth Confusion

recommendations: Reduce central chokepoints and shorten synchronous dependency paths.
summary: The architecture contains centralized or blocking components that limit growth.
title: Scalability Bottlenecks

recommendations: Introduce isolation boundaries around bridge nodes and cycle-heavy paths.
summary: Failures in one component easily spread to others, causing cascading outages.
title: Weak Fault Isolation

recommendations: Add logging, metrics, or tracing to poorly instrumented modules.
summary: The system was not designed with sufficient logging, metrics, tracing, and diagnosability.
title: Lack of Observability

recommendations: Make trust boundaries and security-sensitive paths explicit.
summary: Authentication, authorization, trust boundaries, secret handling, and data protection are treated as afterthoughts.
title: Security Not Built Into the Architecture
